EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of November, 2014. Exceptional drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for almost the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

The Banner Fire started around 0930 PST on July 3 in the Banner, California area, near Route 78 in San Diego County. It quickly spread westward and expanded to 150 acres, threatening the town of Julian. Portions of Route 78 were closed, and mandatory evacuation was ordered for 200 homes; however, the evacuation order was lifted later that evening, as the containment increased. Two homes were destroyed. On July 4, four injuries were reported. On July 6, at 0815 PST, the Banner Fire was 100% contained. However, the road closures still remained in effect through the day, as the firefighting crews continued to monitor the burn areas for potential flare-ups. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Courtney fire was located 3 miles East of Oakhurst in Madera County and burned toward the community of Bass Lake. The fire began on September 14, 2014 and burned 320 acres before being contained on September 22, 2014. The cause of the fire was under investigation, but is believed to be human caused. The fire resulted in evacuations of several hundred residents and visitors for several days. There were 30 residences, 19 outbuildings, 13 vehicles, and 4 recreational vehicles destroyed as well as 4 residences, 3 outbuildings and 2 vehicles damaged. Cost of containment was $4.4 million.

A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley of the Falls Road reported open, but minor flooding as Mill Creek was running full. Yucaipa Ridge reported 4 inches of rain in 6 hours.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind storm struck the Bay Area on December 30 during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong winds brought down numerous trees and power lines across the area. The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blown down blocking the Interstate 880 South off ramp onto East Brokaw near Milpitas.

A powerful early-season winter storm moves across Southwestern California on Halloween night. The storm produced some significant rainfall with amounts in the coastal areas ranging from 0.25 to 1.50 while the mountains received up to 2.00. In the Camarillo area, near the Springs burn scar, a mud/debris flow occurred. Otherwise just some minor nuisance flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate showers produced a mud and debris flow on the 6400 block of San Como Lane in the community of Camarillo. The debris flow occurred near the burn scar of the Springs Fire. Two homes were damaged by mud and one man was trapped for some time in waist-deep mud.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CVS pharmacy in the Felton Fair Shopping Center at 6247 Graham Hill Road was closed after a 15 foot section of roof collapsed due the weight of heavy rain. This also broke all the sprinkler pipes and the combination of rainwater and sprinkler water flooded the inside of the store.

The Nicholls wildfire started on July 11, 2014 and was contained on July 19, 2014. The fire was located 26 miles west of Ridgecrest in Kern County. It burned 1680 acres. There were no structures were lost and no fatalities. The cost of containment was $7.0 million.
An unseasonably cold system brought several inches of snow to the northern Sierra, impacting traffic on roads above 6000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 5 inches of new snow reported at Kingvale 6100 feet and at Soda Springs 6700 feet, along with gusty winds.  Chain controls were required, and traffic slow downs were reported on Interstate 80.

The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

The El Portal wildfire began July 26, 2014 and was contained on August 4, 2014, 1 mile east of El Portal in Mariposa County. The fire burned 4689 acres, of which 1092 acres were in Yosemite National Park and 3597 acres in Stanislaus National Forest. The cause is under investigation. There were 2 structures lost and no fatalities. Cost of containment was $10.6 million.

The Dark Hole wildfire started on July 16, 2014 by lightning. It was being managed for resource benefit, but began being actively contained after the El Portal fire started nearby on July 26, 2014. It was fully contained on August 19, 2014. The fire was located 4 miles north of Yosemite Valley in Mariposa County. It burned 1080 acres. There were no structures lost and no fatalities. The cost of containment is unknown.

A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported flooding and mud at San Timoteo Canyon Road and Live Oak Canyon Road.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous beach conditions were present during the weekend of October 11-12. Several rescues took place at area beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were fishing on rocks in the Marin Headlands. A sneaker wave swept them off the rocks south of Rodeo Beach and into the Ocean. The Coast Guard, National Park Service, and Southern Marin figherfighters responded. They found one individual clinging to rocks and rescued him and he was treated for hypothermia. One victim was found by a Coast Guard boat in the water, deceased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous beach conditions were present during the weekend of October 11-12. Several rescues took place at area beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people were on the beach at Gray Whale Cove when a Sneaker Wave washed them out into the ocean. A dozen beachgoers formed a human chain and pulled the three from the water. Emergency responders performed CPR on a woman pulled from the surf. She was airlifted to a hospital but died. The other two people rescued were treated for hypothermia.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous beach conditions occurred at area beaches over the weekend of October 25-26. Two beachgoers were swept into the sea and one did not survive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two beachgoers at Asilomar State Beach were swept into the water by a sneaker wave. A surfer was able to rescue one of the people but could not find the second person. The Coast Guard conducted an extensive search but was not able to locate the second person.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY COASTS INCLUDING DOWNTOWN LOS ANGELES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Marie, which reached Category 5 status, generated a very large southeast swell that impacted the coast of Southern California with high surf, dangerous rip currents and numerous damage to piers, breakwalls and other infrastructure. Based on historical data, this particular level of surf event occurs once every 10 years, but this particular event was the most significant in the last 30 years. Surf heights between 15 and 20 feet were common at the peak of the event. At the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach, initial damages estimates of $20-40 million were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large southeast swells from Hurricane Marie generated high surf conditions along local beaches. Surf heights between 15 and 20 feet were reported at local beaches, including Cabrillo and Malibu. The swell and surf generated significant rip current activity which resulted in over 300 rescues by Los Angeles county lifeguards. At the time of high tide, some minor coastal flooding occurred including the flooding of some apartments in Seal Beach. Officials at the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach made initial damage estimates between 20 and 40 million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large southeast swells from Hurricane Marie generated high surf conditions along local beaches. Surf heights between 15 and 20 feet were reported at local beaches, including Port Hueneme. The swell and surf generated significant rip current activity which resulted in numerous rescues by local lifeguards. Due to the large swell and surf, local piers experienced some damage including the pier at Port Hueneme.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moved inland over central California, bringing strong onshore flow to southern California and resulting in strong and gusty west to southwest winds in the mountains and deserts of Riverside County during the early morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitewater RAWS measured a peak gust of 59 mph between 0050 and 0150 PST on the 27th. Winds had gusted in the mid to upper 50s for the past 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moved inland over central California, bringing strong onshore flow to southern California and resulting in strong and gusty west to southwest winds in the mountains and deserts of Riverside County during the early morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Burns Canyon RAWS between 2300 PST on the 26th and 0200 PST on the 27th, with southwest winds sustained between 30 and 40 mph and gusting to over 60 mph. The peak gust of 66 mph was recorded between 0000 and 0100 PST on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure deepened along the West Coast deepened the marine layer, with precipitation beginning late evening on the 31st, continuing into November 1. Gusty west winds also affected the mountains and deserts, with a few reaching high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Volcan Mountain mesonet station between 2200-2300 PST. During this time, winds were sustained out of the west-southwest between 35 -45 mph, with a peak gust of 63 mph.

CZ_NAME: IMPERIAL COUNTY EXCEPT THE LOWER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across south central Imperial county during the early afternoon hours on August 21st, and the storms affected the communities of Imperial and El Centro. The storms initially produced strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph, and the winds stirred up significant amounts of dust and sand, leading to dust storm conditions in portions of El Centro. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the blowing dust, and no Dust Storm Warnings were issued. Heavy thunderstorm rains soon followed and ended the dust storm threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms developed across south central Imperial county during the early afternoon hours on August 21st, and the storms affected the communities of Imperial and El Centro. Initially, the storms produced strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph; the gusty winds stirred up significant amounts of dust and sand and produced dust storm conditions. According to local law enforcement, at 1300PST visibility dropped to approximately zero miles in El Centro due to a dust storm. Shortly thereafter, heavy rains followed and eliminated the dust storm threat. No Dust Storm Warnings or Blowing Dust Advisories were issued for the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted the Bay Area with rain and wind, bringing some flooding issues as well as downed trees and power lines across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomically high tide, combined with strong onshore winds, elevated water levels due to an approaching storm surge, and ongoing heavy rainfall all combined to create high impacts from coastal flooding in Marin County. Three lanes of southbound US Highway 101 just north of Sausalito were closed due to flooding. This resulted in significant travel delays and major disruptions to the morning commute into San Francisco from Marin County. Marin County law enforcement asked all residents in Mill Valley to stay home due to the significance of the coastal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding of the small creek Arroyo San Jose into the Los Robles Mobile Home Park in Novato. Minor roadway erosion occurred on roads in the park nearest the creek. Minor damage to mobile homes was reported but the waters receded quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted the Bay Area with rain and wind, bringing some flooding issues as well as downed trees and power lines across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 foot tree was downed by strong winds across Zayante Road near Newton Drive. The tree also fell on several cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind storm struck the Bay Area on December 30 during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong winds brought down numerous trees and power lines across the area. The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four foot diameter tree was blown down blocking both lanes of Empire Grade Road and resulting in a closure of the road.

A 3 to 4 foot south swell with a period of 20 to 23 seconds brought high surf ans strong rip currents along the south-facing beaches of Orange and northern San Diego Counties. Huntington Beach lifeguards reported 8 to 10 foot surf along with wide and powerful rip currents on the 7th. A lifeguard fatality was reported during a rescue in high surf and strong rip currents at Newport Beach, time unknown. The lifeguard was one of the ones that works on patrol boats. Heavy rain showers and thunderstorms brought record rainfall and flooding issues to portions of the Central Valley and foothills. There were 2 berm levees which failed in Tehama County, flooding over 200 homes and damaging farms and orchards. Significant traffic delays were caused by road flooding across interior Northern California. Snow levels remained above 7500 feet, so snowfall was limited to higher Sierra peaks and Lassen Peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway at Coleman Fish Hatchery Rd. and Gover Rd completely flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event beginning early December 11th brought periods of heavy rainfall, flooding from overly saturated grounds and high river/stream water levels, and heavy snow above 7000 ft. Windy conditions persisted through the event, causing trees and tree branches to fall down and localized power outages affecting hundreds of thousands of homes across Northern California. Snowfall of 1 to 2 feet caused travel problems at Sierra passes. Winds were reported to reach as high as 50-60 mph in the valley/foothill region, and over 100 mph in the mountains. |On December 22, Governor Brown declared a state of emergency for heavy rain, flooding and wind damage in counties including Tehama, Lake, Shasta, and Yolo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two apartment buildings suffered flooding as a creek behind the buildings over topped.  Residents were forced to evacuate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist conditions from recent rains couple with light winds brought areas of dense fog to the Central Valley and into the adjacent foothills. This caused minor car accidents and travel delays, especially affecting holiday travelers. A small engine plane crash under investigation by the National Transportation Safety Board is thought to have been related to reduced visibility from the fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single engine plane (Cessna 172) crashed on a remote hillside between Gopher Flat Road and Amador Road, just north of Sutter Creek, in Amador County, CA. There were 2 adult male fatalities. The plane was flying from Gilroy, CA to Sutter Creek, CA. Foggy conditions were present at Auburn Airport to the north, between 11:55 PST and 13:15 PST. Reduced visibility down to around 2 miles was recorded at Columbia Airport to the south at 11:35 PST. The exact time of the crash is currently unknown, but Amador County deputies received reports of the downed plane just after noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to the Sierra Nevada, the foothills, and the Valley. Butte County received significant wind damage, with 2 fatalities caused by falling trees. A fallen tree also caused a fatality and several injuries in Shasta County. Multiple fallen trees caused damage to homes in the Motherlode foothills and in the Sacramento metro area. Fallen trees and branches also caused power outages, with 344,000 customers across northern California impacted. The storm dropped several inches of snow at higher elevations, causing traffic slowdowns and chain controls over the Sierra passes. Strong winds and cold temperatures also combined to bring wind chill temperatures well below zero for the higher Sierra, impacting outdoor recreation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds. A tree fell on a vehicle on Elliot Road in the city of Paradise at 7:48 am. This caused an adult female fatality. Elliott Road was closed from Ingalls Road to Copeland Road for a time, while crews responded to the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 17th, 2014, Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California. The drought continued through the year 2014 with well below normal precipitation, though there were brief periods of relief in February/March and December from heavy rains. Exceptional drought was reported by the U.S. Drought Monitor by the end of July for all of Interior Northern California, with the most impacted area being the Northern San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts to the region included: Reservoirs dropped to low levels (record lows in some cases), farmers limited plantings of some crops and allowed areas of land to go fallow, increased agricultural unemployment and poverty, strong conservation efforts within many communities, increased reliance on shrinking groundwater resources, a decrease in snow-related tourism activity in the Northern Sierra Nevada, and a large increase in fire danger across the Northern Sierra Nevada, Southern Cascades and Coastal Range. Large wildfires such as the King Fire required large scale and very expensive suppression efforts. |For the entire state of California, 2014 was the warmest on record, which further increased the need for water and reduced an already limited snow pack. The Sierra snow pack ended the year at only about half of normal levels.|The California Department of Food and Agriculture���s Office of Public Relations summarized 2014 California agricultural losses: Direct costs to agriculture total $1.5 billion (revenue losses of $1 billion and $0.5 billion in additional pumping costs). This net revenue loss is about 3 percent of the state���s total agricultural value. The total statewide economic cost of the 2014 drought is $2.2 billion. The loss of 17,100 seasonal and part-time jobs related to agriculture represents 3.8 percent of farm unemployment. About 428,000 acres, or 5 percent, of irrigated cropland went out of production in the Central Valley, Central Coast and Southern California due to the drought.|The UC Davis Center for Watershed Sciences research suggests that northern interior California lost 4115 to 6796 agricultural jobs in 2014. Almond production dropped by 15 percent compared to the 2013. Almonds are the largest crop for the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of large, long-period northwest swells, generated by storms moving over the north-central Pacific brought high surf and strong rip currents to portions of the Orange and San Diego County coast during the week of January 20th. Areas north of Newport Beach in Orange County and south of Del Mar in San Diego County saw widespread 5 to 8 foot surf with sets to 10 feet. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and potent low pressure system approached southern California on the 30th with showers increasing through the day and night with a shot of very low-elevation snow. The brunt of the storm occurred during the night of the 30th and morning of the 31st. Snow levels dropped rapidly overnight down to 1500-2000 feet, with some light accumulations even down to 1000 feet. The southern Inland Empire was hardest hit with the unusually low snow, covering areas like Temecula, Lake Elsinore, and Wildomar, which caused issues all along Interstate 15 down through Valley Center. Several inches were also reported in Ranchita and Julian in San Diego County. Overall, the snow totals went as follows: 10 to 18 inches across Palomar Mountain to Julian, 6 to 9 inches from Warner Springs to Julian in San Diego County, 2 to 4 inches from Temecula to Wildomar and Lake Elsinore in the southern Inland Empire, and 4 to 8 inches across Highway 74 and Horsethief canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains. The snow resulted in several road closures, including Highway 74 and Highway 18, stranded vehicles and chain controls on the roads that remained open. Rainfall totals below the snow level generally ranged from .25-1.25 inches, with no significant flooding reported. Thunderstorms also occurred with the leading edge of the cold front from Oceanside and Carlsbad to La Mesa with hail up to one-half inch diameter. Strong winds also accompanied this system with several reports of damage. Most gusts were sub-warning criteria, though a few stations recorded gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in El Cariso Village reported 10 inches of snow as of 0800 PST with tree damage and power outages. The nearby El Cariso mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 44 mph between 1100-1115 PST on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving across central California brought strong west winds to the mountains and deserts from the evening of the 30th through the morning of the 31st. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts to 60 mph...locally higher were observed. Burns Canyon in the upper desert and Sky Valley in the Coachella Valley were the big hot spots. In addition, high astronomical tides and elevated surf due to the storm resulted in minor coastal flooding near Imperial Beach in San Diego County, closing a couple streets.

The southern Inland Empire was hardest hit with the unusually low snow, covering areas like Temecula, Lake Elsinore, and Wildomar, which caused issues all along Interstate 15 down through Valley Center. Several inches were also reported in Ranchita and Julian in San Diego County. Overall, the snow totals went as follows: 10 to 18 inches across Palomar Mountain to Julian, 6 to 9 inches from Warner Springs to Julian in San Diego County, 2 to 4 inches from Temecula to Wildomar and Lake Elsinore in the southern Inland Empire, and 4 to 8 inches across Highway 74 and Horsethief canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains. The snow resulted in several road closures, including Highway 74 and Highway 18, stranded vehicles and chain controls on the roads that remained open. Rainfall totals below the snow level generally ranged from .25-1.25 inches, with no significant flooding reported. Thunderstorms also occurred with the leading edge of the cold front from Oceanside and Carlsbad to La Mesa with hail up to one-half inch diameter. Strong winds also accompanied this system with several reports of damage. Most gusts were sub-warning criteria, though a few stations recorded gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter just northwest of Lake Elsinore reported 6 inches of snow at 1400 feet causing widespread damage to trees with many limbs broken. CHP reported Interstate 15 impacted by snow with 1-2 at the Clinton Keith Road exit. The Baxter Road off-ramp was closed due to snow accumulations where cars are stuck in snow. Bundy Canyon Road off-ramp Was completely snowed in with several vehicles stuck in the snow. A spotter reported Interstate 15 shutdown at Wildomar with 8-10 inches estimated at elevation of 1400 ft. A member of the public reported a 24 hour snowfall of 5 inches reported near Lake Matthews.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to the Sierra Nevada, the foothills, and the Valley. Butte County received significant wind damage, with 2 fatalities caused by falling trees. A fallen tree also caused a fatality and several injuries in Shasta County. Multiple fallen trees caused damage to homes in the Motherlode foothills and in the Sacramento metro area. Fallen trees and branches also caused power outages, with 344,000 customers across northern California impacted. The storm dropped several inches of snow at higher elevations, causing traffic slowdowns and chain controls over the Sierra passes. Strong winds and cold temperatures also combined to bring wind chill temperatures well below zero for the higher Sierra, impacting outdoor recreation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees reported down in the Sacramento area. Trees were reported falling on homes and business in Sacramento, Elk Grove, and Folsom. Power lines were downed in multiple locations, causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stable conditions under a ridge of high pressure resulted in areas of dense fog developing in the San Joaquin Valley each night from December 7 through 11. ||On the evening of December 7, the dense fog began in the center of the valley from Merced to Hanford, then spread throughout the valley after midnight with widespread visibility less and 1/4 mile except at Fresno and Bakersfield. The fog cleared by mid-morning on December 8. ||Dense fog redeveloped quickly after sunset on December 8, and became widespread during the evening, including the Fresno and Bakersfield areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of December, 2014. Although there was heavy rain and snowfall across central California in early December, the later part of December was mainly dry. The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving across central California brought strong west winds to the mountains and deserts from the evening of the 30th through the morning of the 31st. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts to 60 mph...locally higher were observed. Burns Canyon in the upper desert and Sky Valley in the Coachella Valley were the big hot spots. In addition, high astronomical tides and elevated surf due to the storm resulted in minor coastal flooding near Imperial Beach in San Diego County, closing a couple streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding occurred due to a combination of high astronomical tides and elevated surf. This resulted in Descanso street at South Seacoast Drive being shut down. Cortez Avenue and Palm Avenue also had minor flooding. Tides peaked at 7.7 feet at 0800 PST on the 30th, according to the San Diego tide gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet system moved through Northern California.  Snow levels were higher than normal...but areas above the snow level did receive heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The snow level was above populated areas for this event.  However, the Sand Flat SNOTEL at 6750 feet recorded 6.2 inches of snow during this ten hour interval, and 8.5 inches in the 24 hours ending at 07/2200 PST.  Based on this report and other reports of liquid precipitation exceeding 1.0 inches in 24 hours, it is assumed that Heavy Snow Warning criteria was met in this zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and potent low pressure system approached southern California on the 30th with showers increasing through the day and night with a shot of very low-elevation snow. The brunt of the storm occurred during the night of the 30th and morning of the 31st. Snow levels dropped rapidly overnight down to 1500-2000 feet, with some light accumulations even down to 1000 feet. The southern Inland Empire was hardest hit with the unusually low snow, covering areas like Temecula, Lake Elsinore, and Wildomar, which caused issues all along Interstate 15 down through Valley Center. Several inches were also reported in Ranchita and Julian in San Diego County. Overall, the snow totals went as follows: 10 to 18 inches across Palomar Mountain to Julian, 6 to 9 inches from Warner Springs to Julian in San Diego County, 2 to 4 inches from Temecula to Wildomar and Lake Elsinore in the southern Inland Empire, and 4 to 8 inches across Highway 74 and Horsethief canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains. The snow resulted in several road closures, including Highway 74 and Highway 18, stranded vehicles and chain controls on the roads that remained open. Rainfall totals below the snow level generally ranged from .25-1.25 inches, with no significant flooding reported. Thunderstorms also occurred with the leading edge of the cold front from Oceanside and Carlsbad to La Mesa with hail up to one-half inch diameter. Strong winds also accompanied this system with several reports of damage. Most gusts were sub-warning criteria, though a few stations recorded gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Mt. Laguna HPWREN mesonet between 0315-0515 PST on the 30th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the west-southwest at 45-55 mph with gusts in the low 60s. The peak gust of 63 mph was recorded between 0505-0515 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from a nearly stationary precipitation band brought flash flooding to Tehama County. Local creeks over topped their banks and levees, causing damage to homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused a hard closure of Highway 36 at Baker Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system brought increasing wind and precipitation with a period of heavy rain the morning of the 12th and showers continued into the following morning. Significant post-frontal showers occurred in San Diego County, as well as local convergence of air around coastal islands, producing a narrow band of showers. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were recorded in the coast and valley areas. The snow level dropped to around 6000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of snow above 6000 feet and 6 to 8 inches above 7000 feet. Mountain locations below 6000 feet saw up to around 4 inches of rain. River rises in the San Diego River at Fashion Valley resulted in a levee breach which flooded the parking lot of Qualcomm Stadium. Several other roadways in San Diego County were closed due to flooding with mud and debris in the road, especially near the Tijuana River Valley. Most wind gusts were below 58 mph, with the exception of Burns Canyon in the upper desert. High Surf was also generated along west facing Orange and San Diego County beaches by the storm that moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Solana Beach lifeguards reported 5 to 8 foot surf.  Oceanside Beach lifeguards reported 5 to 7 foot surf. Mission Beach lifeguards reported 6 to 8 foot surf. Moonlight Beach lifeguards reported 6 to 8 foot surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of December, 2014. Although there was heavy rain and snowfall across central California in early December, the later part of December was mainly dry. The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

A storm system that moved through just before Christmas brought gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern county as well as the west side of the San Joaquin Valley early in the morning on December 25. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph reported in the desert areas of Kern county. Following this system, colder air moved into the San Joaquin Valley, resulting in below freezing temperatures on the morning of December 27. Temperatures dropped to 29 to 32 degrees for several hours, however, no crop damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread temperatures 30-32 degrees. No crop damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure off the west coast along with a low pressure system dropping south through the inter-mountain west brought light precipitation to the Sierra Nevada on the 29th and 30th, with some rain and snow in the Kern county mountains as well. Although only a few inches of snow fell over the mountains of Kern county, it was enough to cause the closure of Interstate 5 by late afternoon on December 30. The snow also caused ice and snow covered roads on Highway 166 near Grocers Grade near the Kern-San Luis Obispo county line. This system also brought strong northeast winds over the Sierra Nevada on December 30 and 31, resulting in trees being blown down, power outages, and a ski lift being disabled, stranding 40 people on the lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of wind gusts 35 to 49 mph at RAWs stations with higher gusts estimated due to wind damage. Winds blew down trees and knocked down powerlines near Bass Lake and in Yosemite National Park. Strong winds disabled a ski lift at China Peak Ski Resort near Huntington Lake, stranding 40 people on the ski lift for 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, upper-level trough of low pressure moved through the West on the 16th, with scattered showers and high elevation snow. Showers lingered through the night, before a second, stronger trough moved through the region on the 17th. Showers increased in coverage and intensity with the second trough, behind a leading line of strong thunderstorms, with scattered thunderstorms behind it. Significant lightning activity occurred with this initial, organized line of thunderstorms between approximately 2200 PST on the 16th and 0400 PST on the 17th. Post-frontal showers continued, with a few reports of small hail, and a funnel cloud at Riverside March Field. Two-day rainfall totals varied widely with the coast and valley locations receiving between one-tenth and almost one inch of rain, but most saw between one-quarter and three-quarters of an inch. Coastal mountain slope locations received up to 1.3 inches of rain, while the deserts ranged from a few hundredths of an inch to one-third of an inch (highest amounts in the upper deserts). Several roads were closed due to flooding in San Marcos, Spring Valley and a mud/debris flow along Highway 91 in Corona. The snow level lowered to 4500-5000 feet, with two-day accumulations of up to 4 inches below 6000 feet and up to 10 inches between 6000-8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surfline reported 5 to 8 foot surf at Blacks Beach and 5 to 7 foot surf at Ocean Beach.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind storm struck the Bay Area on December 30 during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong winds brought down numerous trees and power lines across the area. The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch was blown down blocking roadway lanes on Pruneridge avenue and San Tomas Expressway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and associated atmospheric river moved across central California December 2-3, 2014. This storm generated significant rainfall across the lower elevations. One-third to three-quarters of an inch of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and adjacent foothills, with 1 to 3 inches of rain along the west slopes of the Sierra Nevada below the snow line, which was generally above 8000 feet. Heaviest rain fell across Tulare County and Kern County in and around the Lake Isabella area. Up to two feet of snow fell in the high elevations of the Sierra Nevada. While there was significant concern about debris flows on burn scars due to the heavy rain, no problems were noted as rainfall intensity was not great enough. Strong winds were also observed at the base of the Grapevine along I-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 22.8 inches at West Woodchuck Meadow and 24.5 inches at Agnew Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong thunderstorms occurred over the San Bernardino and Riverside County mountains overnight on the 21st, with several lightning strikes which started smoldering wildfires. On the 21st, with the onset of offshore winds, two areas ignited into the Strawberry Fire a few miles southwest of Lake Arrowhead, and the North Face Fire in the foothills north of Mount San Jacinto. These fires were quickly extinguished by firefighters, burning no more than a few acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning on the 17th eventually ignited the Strawberry Fire just southwest of Lake Arrowhead, with the aid of offshore winds.

The California drought continued in full force during the month of December, 2014. Although there was heavy rain and snowfall across central California in early December, the later part of December was mainly dry. The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

A strong cold front moved into central California on Thursday, December 11 and continued to impact the region through December 13. This system brought strong southerly winds across the Tehachapi mountains and into the San Joaquin Valley from the afternoon of the 11th to the morning of the 12th. During this time, winds gusted 70 to 90 mph across the mountains of Kern county with one gust of 105 mph reported at Grapevine Peak RAWS. In the San Joaquin Valley, winds gusted 40 to 60 mph, with the strongest winds occurring across Merced county south through western Fresno county and throughout Kern county. There was widespread minor wind damage across Kern county, including trees toppled, power poles down, and fences blown over. One report included approximately 2000 almond trees down northwest of Bakersfield, with a valued loss of $0.2M-0.5M. It was reported that 9,000 people lost power in the Bakersfield area during the storm. ||This storm system also brought heavy rain and snow beginning late on December 11 and continuing through December 13. Rainfall amounts were generally 1 to 3 inches across the San Joaquin Valley, the lower elevations of the Sierra Nevada, the Sierra Foothills, and the mountains of Kern county. Even the desert portion of Kern county received a tenth of an inch to as much as 1.5 inches of rain. The heavy rain resulted in widespread nuisance flooding, notably in Gustine, the Fresno area, and the Bakersfield area. A portion of Highway 99 (Taft Highway underpass) was closed for a period due to flooding during the morning of Friday, December 12. While a Flood Watch was issued for the Rim Fire burn scar, no impacts were noted. ||Snow was reported as low as 3600 feet where up to 2 inches of snow fell. Otherwise snow amounts ranged from about 8 inches to as much as 20 inches in the Sierra Nevada. NOHRSC analysis showed average snow water equivalent (SWE) in the Sierra increased from 0.4 to 1.1 and area covered by snow was up to 55.7% from 16.3%. A few inches of snow also fell across parts of Kern county. ||As the storm departed, there areas of dense fog developed in the San Joaquin Valley, becoming widespread on December 13 and 14. Another storm system moved into the region on Monday, December 15. Although much weaker than the previous storm, it produced gusty winds in the Kern county mountains and light snow in the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds developed ahead of a strong cold front on December 11. This resulted in sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with widespread gusts over 35 mph. Strongest gusts were recorded and the Bakersfield ASOS with a gust to 55 mph at 1140 pm on December 11. There were numerous reports of trees down in and around Bakersfield, McFarland, and Shafter. In Shafter, around 2000 almond trees were downed.  Power was also lost in parts of Bakersfield due to downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and potent low pressure system approached southern California on the 30th with showers increasing through the day and night with a shot of very low-elevation snow. The brunt of the storm occurred during the night of the 30th and morning of the 31st. Snow levels dropped rapidly overnight down to 1500-2000 feet, with some light accumulations even down to 1000 feet. The southern Inland Empire was hardest hit with the unusually low snow, covering areas like Temecula, Lake Elsinore, and Wildomar, which caused issues all along Interstate 15 down through Valley Center. Several inches were also reported in Ranchita and Julian in San Diego County. Overall, the snow totals went as follows: 10 to 18 inches across Palomar Mountain to Julian, 6 to 9 inches from Warner Springs to Julian in San Diego County, 2 to 4 inches from Temecula to Wildomar and Lake Elsinore in the southern Inland Empire, and 4 to 8 inches across Highway 74 and Horsethief canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains. The snow resulted in several road closures, including Highway 74 and Highway 18, stranded vehicles and chain controls on the roads that remained open. Rainfall totals below the snow level generally ranged from .25-1.25 inches, with no significant flooding reported. Thunderstorms also occurred with the leading edge of the cold front from Oceanside and Carlsbad to La Mesa with hail up to one-half inch diameter. Strong winds also accompanied this system with several reports of damage. Most gusts were sub-warning criteria, though a few stations recorded gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 2051 PST on the 30th and 1051 PST on the 31st. During this time, winds were sustained out of the ENE at 35-55 mph with gusts in the 60s and 70s mph. A peak gust of 79 mph was recorded between 0451-0551 PST on the 31st.

A strong Pacific low pressure system brought increasing wind and precipitation with a period of heavy rain the morning of the 12th and showers continued into the following morning. Significant post-frontal showers occurred in San Diego County, as well as local convergence of air around coastal islands, producing a narrow band of showers. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were recorded in the coast and valley areas. The snow level dropped to around 6000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of snow above 6000 feet and 6 to 8 inches above 7000 feet. Mountain locations below 6000 feet saw up to around 4 inches of rain. River rises in the San Diego River at Fashion Valley resulted in a levee breach which flooded the parking lot of Qualcomm Stadium. Several other roadways in San Diego County were closed due to flooding with mud and debris in the road, especially near the Tijuana River Valley. Most wind gusts were below 58 mph, with the exception of Burns Canyon in the upper desert. High Surf was also generated along west facing Orange and San Diego County beaches by the storm that moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Country Club Road webcam shows the road closed and water flowing over roadway at the Escondido Creek crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, upper-level trough of low pressure moved through the West on the 16th, with scattered showers and high elevation snow. Showers lingered through the night, before a second, stronger trough moved through the region on the 17th. Showers increased in coverage and intensity with the second trough, behind a leading line of strong thunderstorms, with scattered thunderstorms behind it. Significant lightning activity occurred with this initial, organized line of thunderstorms between approximately 2200 PST on the 16th and 0400 PST on the 17th. Post-frontal showers continued, with a few reports of small hail, and a funnel cloud at Riverside March Field. Two-day rainfall totals varied widely with the coast and valley locations receiving between one-tenth and almost one inch of rain, but most saw between one-quarter and three-quarters of an inch. Coastal mountain slope locations received up to 1.3 inches of rain, while the deserts ranged from a few hundredths of an inch to one-third of an inch (highest amounts in the upper deserts). Several roads were closed due to flooding in San Marcos, Spring Valley and a mud/debris flow along Highway 91 in Corona. The snow level lowered to 4500-5000 feet, with two-day accumulations of up to 4 inches below 6000 feet and up to 10 inches between 6000-8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The March AFB AOS observer reported a cold air funnel cloud between 1153-1158 PST.

Two-day rainfall totals varied widely with the coast and valley locations receiving between one-tenth and almost one inch of rain, but most saw between one-quarter and three-quarters of an inch. Coastal mountain slope locations received up to 1.3 inches of rain, while the deserts ranged from a few hundredths of an inch to one-third of an inch (highest amounts in the upper deserts). Several roads were closed due to flooding in San Marcos, Spring Valley and a mud/debris flow along Highway 91 in Corona. The snow level lowered to 4500-5000 feet, with two-day accumulations of up to 4 inches below 6000 feet and up to 10 inches between 6000-8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow of rocks and mud several inches deep covered Highway 91 in Corona. All east-bound lanes were closed at 0200 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of December, 2014. Although there was heavy rain and snowfall across central California in early December, the later part of December was mainly dry. The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported a mudslide covering all lanes for 50 yards along Sloan Canyon Road, mud was approximately 18 inches deep.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm system brought locally high winds, rain and snow, and patchy dense fog to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These winds were measured at an elevation of 10,300 feet 3 miles west of Aspendell. The duration of high winds is unknown - the 110 mph was the peak measured gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm system brought locally high winds, rain and snow, and patchy dense fog to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Amboy reported visibility of about 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm system brought locally high winds, rain and snow, and patchy dense fog to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the Bishop area blew down one set of power lines and several trees, one of which landed on a house. The event time is estimated. The Bishop ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 48 mph.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind storm struck the Bay Area on December 30 during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong winds brought down numerous trees and power lines across the area. The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down onto power lines on Landess Avenue East off ramp from Interstate 680 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure helped create stable conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. After the recent rain, the stable conditions led to dense fog formation during the early morning hours on December 21, again on the morning of December 23 and December 24. The fog covered much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley with visibility less than 1/4 mile. In some areas, visibility fell to less than 200 feet. The dense fog impacted the morning commute, including airline traffic at the Fresno-Yosemite International Airport where visibility dropped to 1/16 of a mile on December 21 and 1/8 mile for a few hours on December 23. Several minor accidents were reported by the CHP, but no known injuries.

A weak, upper-level trough of low pressure moved through the West on the 16th, with scattered showers and high elevation snow. Showers lingered through the night, before a second, stronger trough moved through the region on the 17th. Showers increased in coverage and intensity with the second trough, behind a leading line of strong thunderstorms, with scattered thunderstorms behind it. Significant lightning activity occurred with this initial, organized line of thunderstorms between approximately 2200 PST on the 16th and 0400 PST on the 17th. Post-frontal showers continued, with a few reports of small hail, and a funnel cloud at Riverside March Field. Two-day rainfall totals varied widely with the coast and valley locations receiving between one-tenth and almost one inch of rain, but most saw between one-quarter and three-quarters of an inch. Coastal mountain slope locations received up to 1.3 inches of rain, while the deserts ranged from a few hundredths of an inch to one-third of an inch (highest amounts in the upper deserts). Several roads were closed due to flooding in San Marcos, Spring Valley and a mud/debris flow along Highway 91 in Corona. The snow level lowered to 4500-5000 feet, with two-day accumulations of up to 4 inches below 6000 feet and up to 10 inches between 6000-8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed due to flowing water in Escondido Creek over Country Club Drive on low lying crossing. Webcams from SD county flood control confirmed flooding. News 10 reported roadway flooding Along Bent Avenue at Discover Street in San Marcos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into central California on Thursday, December 11 and continued to impact the region through December 13. This system brought strong southerly winds across the Tehachapi mountains and into the San Joaquin Valley from the afternoon of the 11th to the morning of the 12th. During this time, winds gusted 70 to 90 mph across the mountains of Kern county with one gust of 105 mph reported at Grapevine Peak RAWS. In the San Joaquin Valley, winds gusted 40 to 60 mph, with the strongest winds occurring across Merced county south through western Fresno county and throughout Kern county. There was widespread minor wind damage across Kern county, including trees toppled, power poles down, and fences blown over. One report included approximately 2000 almond trees down northwest of Bakersfield, with a valued loss of $0.2M-0.5M. It was reported that 9,000 people lost power in the Bakersfield area during the storm. ||This storm system also brought heavy rain and snow beginning late on December 11 and continuing through December 13. Rainfall amounts were generally 1 to 3 inches across the San Joaquin Valley, the lower elevations of the Sierra Nevada, the Sierra Foothills, and the mountains of Kern county. Even the desert portion of Kern county received a tenth of an inch to as much as 1.5 inches of rain. The heavy rain resulted in widespread nuisance flooding, notably in Gustine, the Fresno area, and the Bakersfield area. A portion of Highway 99 (Taft Highway underpass) was closed for a period due to flooding during the morning of Friday, December 12. While a Flood Watch was issued for the Rim Fire burn scar, no impacts were noted. ||Snow was reported as low as 3600 feet where up to 2 inches of snow fell. Otherwise snow amounts ranged from about 8 inches to as much as 20 inches in the Sierra Nevada. NOHRSC analysis showed average snow water equivalent (SWE) in the Sierra increased from 0.4 to 1.1 and area covered by snow was up to 55.7% from 16.3%. A few inches of snow also fell across parts of Kern county. ||As the storm departed, there areas of dense fog developed in the San Joaquin Valley, becoming widespread on December 13 and 14. Another storm system moved into the region on Monday, December 15. Although much weaker than the previous storm, it produced gusty winds in the Kern county mountains and light snow in the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported across Kern County. Roadway flooding was reported along Interstate 5 at Copus Road. Flooding was also reported along Highway 99 west of Lamont, closing the highway for a few hours. Along highway 178, the heavy rain resulted in debris flow from a recent burn area and closed the east bound lane of the highway at the mouth of the Kern Canyon, east of Bakersfield.

A strong Pacific low pressure system brought increasing wind and precipitation with a period of heavy rain the morning of the 12th and showers continued into the following morning. Significant post-frontal showers occurred in San Diego County, as well as local convergence of air around coastal islands, producing a narrow band of showers. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were recorded in the coast and valley areas. The snow level dropped to around 6000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of snow above 6000 feet and 6 to 8 inches above 7000 feet. Mountain locations below 6000 feet saw up to around 4 inches of rain. River rises in the San Diego River at Fashion Valley resulted in a levee breach which flooded the parking lot of Qualcomm Stadium. Several other roadways in San Diego County were closed due to flooding with mud and debris in the road, especially near the Tijuana River Valley. Most wind gusts were below 58 mph, with the exception of Burns Canyon in the upper desert. High Surf was also generated along west facing Orange and San Diego County beaches by the storm that moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Burns Canyon RAWS reported high winds between 0151-0451 PST on the 12th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the southwest at 40-50 mph. The peak gust of 76 mph was recorded between 0151-0251 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind storm struck the Bay Area on December 30 during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong winds brought down numerous trees and power lines across the area. The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree blown down into lanes of eastbound route 4 at Morello Avenue off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure helped create stable conditions in the San Joaquin Valley. After the recent rain, the stable conditions led to dense fog formation during the early morning hours on December 21, again on the morning of December 23 and December 24. The fog covered much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley with visibility less than 1/4 mile. In some areas, visibility fell to less than 200 feet. The dense fog impacted the morning commute, including airline traffic at the Fresno-Yosemite International Airport where visibility dropped to 1/16 of a mile on December 21 and 1/8 mile for a few hours on December 23. Several minor accidents were reported by the CHP, but no known injuries.

The California drought continued in full force during the month of December, 2014. Although there was heavy rain and snowfall across central California in early December, the later part of December was mainly dry. The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). For the calendar year, 2014 was the warmest year on record for Fresno and Bakersfield. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains.

Surface pressure strengthened to a 1035 mb high over the Great Basin on the 22nd, resulting in gusty Santa Ana winds through the 23rd, strongest in the early mornings of the 23rd. Winds remained strong through the 25th, but shifted around to the northwest (onshore) on the 24th ahead of an approaching low pressure system passing to the north of the area. No significant damage was reported as a result of these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 0051-0551 PST on the 23rd. During this time, winds were sustained out of the ENE at 30-40 mph with gusts in the 60s, including the peak gust of 67 mph, recorded between 0151-0251 PST. High winds occurred at the Pleasants Peak mesonet station between 0059-0229 PST on the 23rd. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east at 45-50 mph with gusts in the 60s, with a peak gust of 70 mph, recorded between 0129-0159 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported the closure of Quarry Road between Lakeview Avenue and Elkelton Drive due to flooding.

A dry cold front passed through the area overnight on the 15th, into the 16th, with gusty west winds. Behind the front, winds turned northeasterly (Santa Ana winds) and gusty later on the 16th, lasting through the 17th as surface high pressure built in over the Great Basin. Riverside and Orange Counties were the most affected with winds during this event, with a few reports of wind damage in the Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Whitewater RAWS between 1650-1750 PST and ended between 2250-2350 PST on the evening of the 15th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the west-northwest at 35-45 mph with gusts near 60, including the peak gust of 60 mph recorded between 2150-2250 PST.

Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A huge pine tree blown down and blocking an entire roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow caused travel difficulties in the Sierra above 6000 feet. Moderate rain of 1/2 to 2 inches fell in the foothills and lower mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts near 6000 feet were around 6 inches, with up to a foot at Donner Summit at 7200 feet. The slippery roads caused travel difficulties and delays over Sierra passes, aggravated by increased traffic due to the holiday weekend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front passed through the area overnight on the 15th, into the 16th, with gusty west winds. Behind the front, winds turned northeasterly (Santa Ana winds) and gusty later on the 16th, lasting through the 17th as surface high pressure built in over the Great Basin. Riverside and Orange Counties were the most affected with winds during this event, with a few reports of wind damage in the Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Pleasants Peak SDGE mesonet between 0425-0455 PST and ended between 1059-1129 PST on the morning of the 16th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east-northeast at 35-55 mph with gusts over 60 mph. The peak gust of 76 mph recorded between 1029-1059 PST. High winds also occurred at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 0651-0951 PST on the 16th, with sustained winds out of the northeast at 35-40 mph, including the peak gust of 62 mph recorded between 0851-0951 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front passed through the area overnight on the 15th, into the 16th, with gusty west winds. Behind the front, winds turned northeasterly (Santa Ana winds) and gusty later on the 16th, lasting through the 17th as surface high pressure built in over the Great Basin. Riverside and Orange Counties were the most affected with winds during this event, with a few reports of wind damage in the Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred at the Sill Hill and Crestwood SDGE mesonet stations from the evening of the 16th through the early morning of the 17th. During this period, winds were gusting 45 to 55 mph out of the east to northeast. Crestwood recorded a peak gust of 59 mph as well, between 1200-1210 PST on the 16th. Sill Hill recorded a peak gust of 59 mph between 0230-0240 PST on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front passed through the area overnight on the 15th, into the 16th, with gusty west winds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly winds caused damage to a roof in Eastvale, near the city of Riverside. Wind gusts were estimated around 52 mph, based on nearby observations. The Press Enterprise newspaper cited 1400 customers without power in central San Bernardino at 1022 PST on the 16th. CHP also reported power lines down northeast of Perris at 1347 PST and east of Moreno Valley at 2355 PST on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow from a low pressure system moving inland through northern California created gusty west winds along the desert slopes on the 22nd. Strong surface high pressure built over the Great Basin on the 23rd behind the departing upper-level trough, resulting in strong, gusty east to northeast winds (Santa Ana winds) in the mountains and on the coastal mountain slopes. The strongest gusts were focused in San Bernardino and Riverside Counties on the 22nd when winds were out of the west then shifting around to the north on the 23rd, then shifted to San Diego County as winds turned more easterly later on the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Whitewater RAWS between 2100 PST and 0000 PST on the 23rd, with sustained winds out of the west-northwest at 35-45 mph and gusting over 60 mph. The peak gust of 63 mph was recorded between 2050-2150 PST on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface pressure strengthened to a 1035 mb high over the Great Basin on the 22nd, resulting in gusty Santa Ana winds through the 23rd, strongest in the early mornings of the 23rd. Winds remained strong through the 25th, but shifted around to the northwest (onshore) on the 24th ahead of an approaching low pressure system passing to the north of the area. No significant damage was reported as a result of these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Whitewater RAWS between 2200 PST on the 24th and 0400 PST on the 25th. During this time, winds were sustained out of the WNW at 30-40 mph with gusts in the upper 50s, with a peak gust of 61 mph, recorded between 2150-2250 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stable conditions under a ridge of high pressure resulted in areas of dense fog developing in the San Joaquin Valley each night from December 7 through 11. ||On the evening of December 7, the dense fog began in the center of the valley from Merced to Hanford, then spread throughout the valley after midnight with widespread visibility less and 1/4 mile except at Fresno and Bakersfield. Visibility lowered to less than 200 feet at times, resulting in the California Highway Patrol pacing traffic on area highways. The fog cleared by mid-morning on December 8. ||Dense fog redeveloped quickly after sunset on December 8, and became widespread during the evening, including the Fresno and Bakersfield areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and associated atmospheric river moved across central California December 2-3, 2014. This storm generated significant rainfall across the lower elevations. One-third to three-quarters of an inch of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and adjacent foothills, with 1 to 3 inches of rain along the west slopes of the Sierra Nevada below the snow line, which was generally above 8000 feet. Heaviest rain fell across Tulare County and Kern County in and around the Lake Isabella area. Up to two feet of snow fell in the high elevations of the Sierra Nevada. While there was significant concern about debris flows on burn scars due to the heavy rain, no problems were noted as rainfall intensity was not great enough. Strong winds were also observed at the base of the Grapevine along I-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Wishon Dam /WSHC1/ rainfall 1.83 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow marine layer resulted in areas of dense fog along the immediate coast of Orange and San Diego Counties, impacting some of the coastal airports. Pockets of very low visibility also affected portions of Interstates 8 and 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: John Wayne Airport reported one-quarter mile visibility from 0031 to 0101 PST on the 9th. Fullerton Municipal Airport reported one-quarter mile visibility from 0400 to 0743 PST on the 9th. Los Alamitos U.S. Army Airfield reported one-quarter mile or less visibility from 0216 am to 0858 PST on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported that the road needs to be closed due to flooding at Rancho California Road and Anza Road.

The combination of recent heavy rains earlier in the month brought weakened ground conditions and then there were numerous dead trees across the area from years of ongoing drought. Major disruptions to the evening commute due to downed trees and the accidents they caused were observed. Wind gusts of 40-55 MPH were widespread across the area. Despite most areas not reaching 60 MPH, there was widespread wind damage and impacts due to the reasons above.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down by strong winds and blocking Lopez and Sloat roads in all directions.
