EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large west-northwest swell brought elevated surf to west-facing Orange and San Diego County beaches on the 19th and 20th. The highest surf occurred in northern Orange County, near and north of Newport Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surfline reported 5 to 8 foot surf on the 19th and 20th at Huntington Beach, Newport Beach, and Salt Creek Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and associated atmospheric river moved across central California December 2-3, 2014. This storm generated significant rainfall across the lower elevations. One-third to three-quarters of an inch of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and adjacent foothills, with 1 to 3 inches of rain along the west slopes of the Sierra Nevada below the snow line, which was generally above 8000 feet. Heaviest rain fell across Tulare County and Kern County in and around the Lake Isabella area. Up to two feet of snow fell in the high elevations of the Sierra Nevada. While there was significant concern about debris flows on burn scars due to the heavy rain, no problems were noted as rainfall intensity was not great enough. Strong winds were also observed at the base of the Grapevine along I-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Johnsondale /JSNC1/ rainfall 2.60 inches in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into central California on Thursday, December 11 and continued to impact the region through December 13. This system brought strong southerly winds across the Tehachapi mountains and into the San Joaquin Valley from the afternoon of the 11th to the morning of the 12th. During this time, winds gusted 70 to 90 mph across the mountains of Kern county with one gust of 105 mph reported at Grapevine Peak RAWS. In the San Joaquin Valley, winds gusted 40 to 60 mph, with the strongest winds occurring across Merced county south through western Fresno county and throughout Kern county. There was widespread minor wind damage across Kern county, including trees toppled, power poles down, and fences blown over. One report included approximately 2000 almond trees down northwest of Bakersfield, with a valued loss of $0.2M-0.5M. It was reported that 9,000 people lost power in the Bakersfield area during the storm. ||This storm system also brought heavy rain and snow beginning late on December 11 and continuing through December 13. Rainfall amounts were generally 1 to 3 inches across the San Joaquin Valley, the lower elevations of the Sierra Nevada, the Sierra Foothills, and the mountains of Kern county. Even the desert portion of Kern county received a tenth of an inch to as much as 1.5 inches of rain. The heavy rain resulted in widespread nuisance flooding, notably in Gustine, the Fresno area, and the Bakersfield area. A portion of Highway 99 (Taft Highway underpass) was closed for a period due to flooding during the morning of Friday, December 12. While a Flood Watch was issued for the Rim Fire burn scar, no impacts were noted. ||Snow was reported as low as 3600 feet where up to 2 inches of snow fell. Otherwise snow amounts ranged from about 8 inches to as much as 20 inches in the Sierra Nevada. NOHRSC analysis showed average snow water equivalent (SWE) in the Sierra increased from 0.4 to 1.1 and area covered by snow was up to 55.7% from 16.3%. A few inches of snow also fell across parts of Kern county. ||As the storm departed, there areas of dense fog developed in the San Joaquin Valley, becoming widespread on December 13 and 14. Another storm system moved into the region on Monday, December 15. Although much weaker than the previous storm, it produced gusty winds in the Kern county mountains and light snow in the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred ahead of a strong cold front on December 11, 2014. The strongest winds occurred at the Grapevine Peak RAWS where sustained winds were measured 50 to 76 mph with frequent gusts over 75 mph. Peak wind gust was 105 mph. Elsewhere across the mountains of Kern County, sustained winds were generally 30 to 45 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph with a few places gusting over 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stable conditions under a ridge of high pressure resulted in areas of dense fog developing in the San Joaquin Valley each night from December 7 through 11. ||On the evening of December 7, the dense fog began in the center of the valley from Merced to Hanford, then spread throughout the valley after midnight with widespread visibility less and 1/4 mile except at Fresno and Bakersfield. Visibility lowered to less than 200 feet at times, resulting in the California Highway Patrol pacing traffic on area highways. The fog cleared by mid-morning on December 8. ||Dense fog redeveloped quickly after sunset on December 8, and became widespread during the evening, including the Fresno and Bakersfield areas. Parts of Kings and Kern counties had near zero visibility. There were many school bus delays due to the fog. The dense fog lasted until around noon. ||The was a repeat of the dense fog on the evening of December 9 lasting until mid-morning on December 10. As a storm system approached the area bringing clouds across the region on afternoon of December 10, dense fog was limited only to the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, where visibility in Kern County fell to less than 1/4 mile for a only few hours, lifting around 2 am.

Strong low pressure approaching the northern California coast brought widespread wind damage to the Lake Tahoe area and to parts of Mono County on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 and 70 mph at the Truckee and South Lake Tahoe airports, respectively, on the morning of the 11th. Over the Sierra ridges, winds gusted as high as 135 mph. Numerous trees and power lines were downed, along with damage to several homes and vehicles due to fallen trees. The power outages, some lasting up to 2 days, caused South Lake Tahoe schools to be closed through the 12th. Finally, winds downed a tree which caused the death of a teenager in a heavily wooded area. Damages from this event are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure approaching the northern California coast brought widespread wind damage to the Lake Tahoe area and to parts of Mono County on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downslope winds gusted to between 65 and 80 mph near the highway 395 corridor. Locally higher winds to over 100 mph occurred near the highway 395 and state route 167 junction (near Mono City) shortly after 1100PST, with reports of windows shattered and roof shingles blown off. Also, a roof blew off a home and serious damage was noted at a gas station awning in Lee Vining.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought moderate to heavy rain and flash flooding to the region from the 2nd through the 4th. Two-day rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were recorded west of the mountains, while the southern slopes of the San Bernardino County mountains saw up to 5 inches of rain (isolated amount of 14.5 inches at Yucaipa Ridge). The heavy rain caused countless instances of flooding, with mud, debris and water closing several roadways and stranding vehicles. Snow levels remained above 8000 feet, so no snow accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP and ABC 7 news reported Highway 74 between Dartmouth and Columbia closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that moved through just before Christmas brought gusty winds across the mountains and desert areas of Kern county as well as the west side of the San Joaquin Valley early in the morning on December 25. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph reported in the desert areas of Kern county. Following this system, colder air moved into the San Joaquin Valley, resulting in below freezing temperatures on the morning of December 27. Temperatures dropped to 29 to 32 degrees for several hours, however, no crop damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mojave Airport reported gusts over 65 mph with a peak gust of 75 mph.

A strong Pacific low pressure system brought increasing wind and precipitation with a period of heavy rain the morning of the 12th and showers continued into the following morning. Significant post-frontal showers occurred in San Diego County, as well as local convergence of air around coastal islands, producing a narrow band of showers. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were recorded in the coast and valley areas. The snow level dropped to around 6000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of snow above 6000 feet and 6 to 8 inches above 7000 feet. Mountain locations below 6000 feet saw up to around 4 inches of rain. River rises in the San Diego River at Fashion Valley resulted in a levee breach which flooded the parking lot of Qualcomm Stadium. Several other roadways in San Diego County were closed due to flooding with mud and debris in the road, especially near the Tijuana River Valley. Most wind gusts were below 58 mph, with the exception of Burns Canyon in the upper desert. High Surf was also generated along west facing Orange and San Diego County beaches by the storm that moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported flooding on Bastanchury Road with streets under up to a foot of standing water.  Flooding was also reported by a spotter at the Miraloma and Lakeview Avenue low water crossing with 6 inches of water on the roadway. A nearby mesonet station in Yorba Linda recorded a 3-hour rain total of 1.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface pressure strengthened to a 1035 mb high over the Great Basin on the 22nd, resulting in gusty Santa Ana winds through the 23rd, strongest in the early mornings of the 23rd. Winds remained strong through the 25th, but shifted around to the northwest (onshore) on the 24th ahead of an approaching low pressure system passing to the north of the area. No significant damage was reported as a result of these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at the Sill Hill SDGE mesonet were sustained 35-45 mph out of the east with gusts in the low to mid-50s mph between about 0600-1000 PST on the 23rd. During this time, a peak gust of 60 mph was recorded between 0630-0640 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure off the west coast along with a low pressure system dropping south through the inter-mountain west brought light precipitation to the Sierra Nevada on the 29th and 30th, with some rain and snow in the Kern county mountains as well. Although only a few inches of snow fell over the mountains of Kern county, it was enough to cause the closure of Interstate 5 by late afternoon on December 30. The snow also caused ice and snow covered roads on Highway 166 near Grocers Grade near the Kern-San Luis Obispo county line. This system also brought strong northeast winds over the Sierra Nevada on December 30 and 31, resulting in trees being blown down, power outages, and a ski lift being disabled, stranding 40 people on the lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 2 to 3 inches fell across parts of the Kern County Mountains, mainly near and west of Interstate 5. Snowy and icy roads resulting in the closure of Interstate 5 over the grapevine overnight. Snowy roads also was a factor in a 15 car pileup along highway 166 over Grocers Grade near the Kern-San Luis Obispo county line. This resulting in the closure of highway 166 for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong thunderstorms occurred over the San Bernardino and Riverside County mountains overnight on the 21st, with several lightning strikes which started smoldering wildfires. On the 21st, with the onset of offshore winds, two areas ignited into the Strawberry Fire a few miles southwest of Lake Arrowhead, and the North Face Fire in the foothills north of Mount San Jacinto. These fires were quickly extinguished by firefighters, burning no more than a few acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from the 17th eventually ignited the North Face Fire on the 21st, with the aid of offshore winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Atmospheric River event brought heavy rain and gusty winds with a strong winter storm that impacted the Bay Area for several days in mid-December. Cyclogenesis occurred with a 979 mb low forming off the Northern California coast. On December 11, the intense surface low moved up the coast and drug a narrow cold frontal rainband (NCFR) through the Bay Area that morning and early afternoon. NCFRs are rapidly moving fronts that are notorious for intense rainfall and gusty winds despite having limited instability. ||The NCFR wasn't a major wind producer, but the rainfall rates were impressive. Instantaneous rainfall rates of 1.5-2 inches/hr were reported. Observational data from the Big Sur Coast were also impressive with Big Sur receiving 4.43 in 2 hours and Flores Camp receiving 3 in 1 hour. It wasn't so much the total amount of rainfall for many locations, but the intensity and how much was received in a short amount of time. ||Many locations around the entire Bay Area had flooding: urban flooding of streets and highways, flooding of creeks and even one large river in the North Bay. Eventually the NCFR slowed around the Big Sur Coast. The stalling was likely due to another 'wave' in the atmosphere, farther to the southwest, riding along the boundary. The end result was to have the weakened NCFR lift back northward, almost like a quasi-warm front, producing another round of moderate to locally heavy rainfall around the Bay Area, compounding flooding concerns. ||In addition to the impressive rainfall, winds also became a problem for the water soaked ground, drought stressed trees and diseased trees. Peak winds occurred over the higher terrain with the Lick Observatory reporting 83 mph. Most low elevation areas saw much lower wind speeds.||Lastly, the intense winds over the coastal waters helped to generate a fresh swell that produced very dangerous surf with large breaking waves. The Big Sur Coast buoy reported a peak wave of 19.7 feet.||The event was followed by several weaker storm systems that week that brought additional rainfall, continued flooding and mudslide concerns to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding on Interstate 280 was reported by the public. This coincided with a nearby observation of 0.90 of rainfall in 30 minutes at San Bruno Mountain. Traffic was significantly impacted and slowed or stopped during the height of morning rush hour.

Observational data from the Big Sur Coast were also impressive with Big Sur receiving 4.43 in 2 hours and Flores Camp receiving 3 in 1 hour. It wasn't so much the total amount of rainfall for many locations, but the intensity and how much was received in a short amount of time. ||Many locations around the entire Bay Area had flooding: urban flooding of streets and highways, flooding of creeks and even one large river in the North Bay. Eventually the NCFR slowed around the Big Sur Coast. The stalling was likely due to another 'wave' in the atmosphere, farther to the southwest, riding along the boundary. The end result was to have the weakened NCFR lift back northward, almost like a quasi-warm front, producing another round of moderate to locally heavy rainfall around the Bay Area, compounding flooding concerns. ||In addition to the impressive rainfall, winds also became a problem for the water soaked ground, drought stressed trees and diseased trees. Peak winds occurred over the higher terrain with the Lick Observatory reporting 83 mph. Most low elevation areas saw much lower wind speeds.||Lastly, the intense winds over the coastal waters helped to generate a fresh swell that produced very dangerous surf with large breaking waves. The Big Sur Coast buoy reported a peak wave of 19.7 feet.||The event was followed by several weaker storm systems that week that brought additional rainfall, continued flooding and mudslide concerns to the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow marine layer resulted in areas of dense fog along the immediate coast of Orange and San Diego Counties, impacting some of the coastal airports. Pockets of very low visibility also affected portions of Interstates 8 and 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montgomery Field reported one-quarter mile visibility from 2249 PST on the 8th to 0253 PST on the 9th. Lindbergh Field reported intermittent one-quarter mile visibility between 0102 and 0315 PST on the 9th. McClellan-Palomar Airport reported one-quarter mile visibility from 2111 PST on the 8th to 0038 PST on the 9th, and again from 0223 to 0351 PST on the 9th.  Imperial Beach Naval Auxiliary Landing Field reported one-quarter mile of visibility from 1956 to 2248 PST on the 8th, and again from 0024 to 0356 PST on the 9th. MCOLF Camp Pendleton at Red Beach reported one-quarter mile visibility from 1802 PST on the 8th to 0021 PST on the 9th, and again from 0256 to 0751 PST on the 9th. North Island NAS reported one-quarter mile visibility between 2108 and 2135 PST on the 8th, and again from 0522 to 0752 PST on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the afternoon bringing locally heavy rain, and hail. There were also strong, gusty winds at times.

NCFRs are rapidly moving fronts that are notorious for intense rainfall and gusty winds despite having limited instability. ||The NCFR wasn't a major wind producer, but the rainfall rates were impressive. Instantaneous rainfall rates of 1.5-2 inches/hr were reported. Observational data from the Big Sur Coast were also impressive with Big Sur receiving 4.43 in 2 hours and Flores Camp receiving 3 in 1 hour. It wasn't so much the total amount of rainfall for many locations, but the intensity and how much was received in a short amount of time. ||Many locations around the entire Bay Area had flooding: urban flooding of streets and highways, flooding of creeks and even one large river in the North Bay. Eventually the NCFR slowed around the Big Sur Coast. The stalling was likely due to another 'wave' in the atmosphere, farther to the southwest, riding along the boundary. The end result was to have the weakened NCFR lift back northward, almost like a quasi-warm front, producing another round of moderate to locally heavy rainfall around the Bay Area, compounding flooding concerns. ||In addition to the impressive rainfall, winds also became a problem for the water soaked ground, drought stressed trees and diseased trees. Peak winds occurred over the higher terrain with the Lick Observatory reporting 83 mph. Most low elevation areas saw much lower wind speeds.||Lastly, the intense winds over the coastal waters helped to generate a fresh swell that produced very dangerous surf with large breaking waves. The Big Sur Coast buoy reported a peak wave of 19.7 feet.||The event was followed by several weaker storm systems that week that brought additional rainfall, continued flooding and mudslide concerns to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ashby Avenue Railway underpass near I-80 is flooded. Three cars are stranded and floating in floodwaters.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous beach conditions were observed across the area on Veteran's Day with one fatality from a sneaker wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people were fishing on rocks at Soberanes Point when a sneaker wave washed one of them out to sea. The Coast Guard and rescue crews attempted to rescue him but could not find him. His body was recovered by rescue divers the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak Pacific storm system moved into the Bay Area, bringing rain and gusty winds to the area on November 29-30. Impacts were not significant but a few trees were downed and minor urban flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds toppled a few trees in the Presidio of San Francisco and brought down powerlines. A large cypress tree fell and brought down adjacent power lines. Fallen live wires forced the closure of Infantry Terrace and Moraga Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ficus tree was snapped by a wind gust. The tree fell on a taxi and broke the car's windshield. The driver was unhurt. Other parked cars nearby sustained minor damage.

Most low elevation areas saw much lower wind speeds.||Lastly, the intense winds over the coastal waters helped to generate a fresh swell that produced very dangerous surf with large breaking waves. The Big Sur Coast buoy reported a peak wave of 19.7 feet.||The event was followed by several weaker storm systems that week that brought additional rainfall, continued flooding and mudslide concerns to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slide under sixty feet of Highway 1 near Muir Beach resulted in a full closure of the road which is expected to last for several months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder Pacific storm brought heavy snow to interior northern California mainly March 31st, but continued into April 1st. Snow levels began around 3000-4000 ft Monday but dropped to 2000-3000 ft by Monday night into Tuesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few inches of snow fell over far Northern Sierra Nevada as a cold Pacific storm moved through the area March 31st through April 1st. Lassen Lodge (4200 ft) received around 6 inches of new snow, and Harkness Flat (6200 ft) received around 4 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the eastern deserts of Riverside County during the evening hours on September 16th. A few of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates reaching at least as high as 1.5 inches per hour. The heavy rain affected locations from Desert Center eastward to Blythe, and caused brief flash flooding which affected motorists on highway 177 near Desert Center. No injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the eastern Riverside County deserts during the evening hours on September 16th, and they produced locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates at least as high as 1.5 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to brief flash flooding along highway 177, about 10 miles to the northeast of Desert Center. According to local law enforcement, the highway was closed for a short time due to high water running over the road. After the thunderstorms dissipated, the high water quickly subsided. There were no reports of stranded motorists or injuries due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across the Bay Area on February 28. It dropped several inches of rainfall and brought gusty winds to the area. This resulted in flooding of urban areas, small streams and creeks, and damage to power lines and trees as well as a few localized mud and rockslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of standing water covering all lanes near Interstate 280 and Highway 87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure in the Great Basin generated strong and gusty north to northeast winds across sections of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains of Los Angeles county as well as the eastern valleys of Ventura county. Additionally, the northeast winds reached offshore to Catalina Island and resulted in the death of two people, including one harbor patrolman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed across Avalon Harbor on Catalina Island. Sustained winds of 36 MPH with gusts to 43 MPH generated very steep and rough waves (4 to 8 feet). The combination of the wind and waves broke the moorings off of several boats and caused other damage. A harbor patrolman was crushed between a boat and some rocks. Another person was found dead, floating in the water.

CZ_NAME: IMPERIAL COUNTY EXCEPT THE LOWER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on December 12th, areas of dense fog developed in the Imperial valley, running the length of the valley from the south shores of the Salton Sea to El Centro. Trained weather spotters reported visibilities reduced to just one quarter of a mile in dense fog. This resulted in hazardous driving conditions during the morning rush hour, and led to the issuance of a Dense Fog Advisory for the Imperial valley. Fortunately, no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed across the Imperial Valley during the early morning hours on December 12th, affecting communities such as Brawley, Calexico, El Centro and Seeley. According to a trained weather spotter located 1 mile south of Seeley, dense fog lowered visibility to 1/4 mile at about 0700PST. The fog persisted into the middle of the morning, creating hazardous driving conditions during the morning rush hour. In addition, the dense fog resulted in the issuance of a Dense Fog Advisory for the Imperial Valley. Fortunately, no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an exceptional period with little to no rainfall, much needed rain fell across the Bay Area February 6th through the 9th. In fact, the lack of rainfall lead to the largest deficit of rain in recorded history for the Bay Area.||The first storm system began to develop on Wednesday (Feb 5) as a shortwave trough moved toward the West Coast. The shortwave trough deepened through the day allowing for a surface low to develop Wednesday Night off the coast. The resulting surface low increased low level warm advection and enhanced orographic lift along the coastal mountains. Rainfall amounts with this system varied from just over one half inch to a little more than two inches. ||The second and much stronger storm system brought impressive rainfall totals to the coastal mountains, especially the North Bay. The bulk of the rainfall occurred Friday night through Saturday. Several key factors led to the impressive rainfall: middle level flow perpendicular to the coast , orographic lift along the coastal mountains, but most prominently was a plume of very moist air streaming in from off the Pacific Ocean (an Atmospheric River).  As the moisture plume moved into the region rain began to overspread the North Bay Thursday night into Friday morning. Over the next 24 hours the main push of higher precipitable water moved over the Bay Area leading to the onset of steadier and at times heavy rainfall. By Sunday, rain began to diminish with off and on showers through the day.||Despite the long preceding period of dry weather, the several days of persistent rainfall resulted in some flooding and even landslides in parts of the Bay Area. Conditions were so bad on a stretch of the Ft Ross-Cazadero Highway that part of it washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle stuck in mud and water, party had to be rescued as they were unable to exit the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm. which began to impact Southern California on the last day in February, continued to bring significant weather to the area through March 2nd. Rainfall totals were very impressive, ranging from 1 to 4 inches across coastal areas to between 6 and 12 inches across the mountains and foothills. This winter storm generated some intense showers which produced flash flooding and debris flows as well as some severe thunderstorms. In the mountains, the combination of 6 to 12 inches of snowfall and southerly wind gusts around 90 MPH generated winter storm conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across the community of Walnut. A trained weather spotter reported 1.50 inch hail from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an exceptional period with little to no rainfall, much needed rain fell across the Bay Area February 6th through the 9th. In fact, the lack of rainfall lead to the largest deficit of rain in recorded history for the Bay Area.||The first storm system began to develop on Wednesday (Feb 5) as a shortwave trough moved toward the West Coast. The shortwave trough deepened through the day allowing for a surface low to develop Wednesday Night off the coast. The resulting surface low increased low level warm advection and enhanced orographic lift along the coastal mountains. Rainfall amounts with this system varied from just over one half inch to a little more than two inches. ||The second and much stronger storm system brought impressive rainfall totals to the coastal mountains, especially the North Bay. The bulk of the rainfall occurred Friday night through Saturday. Several key factors led to the impressive rainfall: middle level flow perpendicular to the coast , orographic lift along the coastal mountains, but most prominently was a plume of very moist air streaming in from off the Pacific Ocean (an Atmospheric River).  As the moisture plume moved into the region rain began to overspread the North Bay Thursday night into Friday morning. Over the next 24 hours the main push of higher precipitable water moved over the Bay Area leading to the onset of steadier and at times heavy rainfall. By Sunday, rain began to diminish with off and on showers through the day.||Despite the long preceding period of dry weather, the several days of persistent rainfall resulted in some flooding and even landslides in parts of the Bay Area. Conditions were so bad on a stretch of the Ft Ross-Cazadero Highway that part of it washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SUV stuck in standing water, reported to be halfway up the door.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of February, 2014. Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for much of the region, with exceptional drought detected along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. For California as a whole, 2013 was the driest year on record. As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

Moisture from the rains saturated the airmass near the San Joaquin Valley floor, bringing the first episodes of Tule Fog of the winter. Of the four days (February 12th-15th) with locally dense fog, the 13th saw the most fog. Even so, the fog was mostly patchy and formed mainly in the Madera-Atwater and Selma-Hanford/Visalia areas. Dense fog also formed near daybreak along the Highway 41 corridor in western Fresno County and the Highway 198 corridor from Lemoore to Hanford.||In addition to the low-level moisture, fog development was aided by a stable airmass due to an upper-level ridge over the central California interior. This high pressure aloft also brought a warming trend, with above-normal temperatures from February 12th through the 17th.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hazardous conditions occurred along beaches of the Bay Area on January 20th due to an incoming long period swell. One man was killed when he drowned near Pt. Reyes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was fishing in Point Reyes National Seashore near the lighthouse. A wave knocked over his ice cooler and carried it into the ocean. He went into the water to attempt to retrieve the cooler and was overcome by large waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous rip currents occurred along the coastline of Central and Northern California. This resulted in water rescues, injured swimmers, and a death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was boogie boarding in Monterey Bay near the Santa Cruz Lighthouse in very rough surf. Lifeguards approached him and attempted to remove him from the area but he did not want assistance. Shortly after, a set of waves slammed him into the rocks. Rescue swimmers went in and were unable to retrieve him due to rough surf - in fact they were injured themselves. A second set of swimmers were able to get the man, and pulled him from the water. He was taken to a local hospital but later died.

A dry storm system moved through the central California interior on February 18th, bringing a push of cold air that lowered temperatures briefly to near normal. Behind the cold front, gusty winds developed over the mountains and deserts on the 19th. The winds began during the early morning hours, with a gust to 60 mph at Bear Peak in Tulare County, and a gust to 53 mph at Mojave, during the pre-dawn hours. Winds gusted over 45 mph in the eastern Kern County desert during the morning and early afternoon of the 19th, and gusts to 50 mph were recorded in the Piutes.||An upper-level ridge of high pressure returned to California on the 19th behind the departing low-pressure system. High temperatures warmed to above normal for the next several days before the next storm arrived during the afternoon of February 26th. This warming trend peaked with Bakersfield hitting 81 degrees on the 25th, and Fresno reaching 80 degrees. This was the first time either city had seen 80 degrees since November 10th, 2013.||The storm that reached the central California interior on February 26th was the first of two closely-spaced systems. Together, the two storms brought as much as 4 inches of rain to the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills, and over an inch of rain to the Kern County deserts. Fresno had a three-day storm total of 1.11 inch, bringing the total rainfall for February to 2.11 inch. This was 0.08 inch greater than the normal of 2.03 inches and was the first time since December 2012 that Fresno had above-normal rainfall for a month.||The south end of the San Joaquin Valley was rain-shadowed, with Bakersfield receiving only 0.28 inch of rain, almost all of which fell from the second storm on February 28th. The second storm was located off the central California coast, and was impressive on satellite pictures with a tightly-wound structure and a clear center much like the eye of a tropical system. The circulation around the storm produced a southerly wind over the Tehachapi Mountains which funneled through Tejon Pass and down the Grapevine into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Winds gusted as high as 89 mph at Grapevine Peak, and to 50 mph at Sunflower Valley on the west side of the Valley. At Buck Rock, in the Sierra Nevada in Tulare County, winds gusted to 66 mph.|Several thunderstorms were spawned by the storms, with the second system being the most potent. Numerous trees were toppled in both the San Joaquin Valley and the Sierra foothills by winds, in many cases pulling down power lines. One thunderstorm at Squaw Valley in the foothills produced a gust estimated at 65 mph, which toppled trees and damaged the roof of a garage. On the Valley floor, there were reports of houses and parked vehicles damaged by falling trees or large limbs. Near Fresno, a tree 3 feet in diameter was toppled.||Street flooding was reported at several locations, not only in the San Joaquin Valley but also in eastern Kern County where the Garlock Road flooded. (Garlock Road is prone to flooding from runoff from the El Paso Mountains.). In Dinuba, water covering the road was estimated at 8 to 12 inches deep. ||In the Southern Sierra Nevada, estimates of new snowfalls were as high as four feet. (These were Snotel observations; a post-storm snow survey had not been conducted at the time of this summary.)||February 2014 was much warmer than normal, and was Fresno���s warmest February on record. Bakersfield tied for its 6th warmest February on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small hail, frequent cloud to ground lightning, wind gusts to 50 mph, and flooded roads along Road 80 just south of Dinuba.  This weather was caused by a cluster of thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the eastern Pacific into the West on the 30th, bringing heavy snow and rain to the eastern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels were initially high (around 7500-8000 feet) as the storm began on the afternoon of the 29th. However, as the storm progressed southeast overnight into the 30th, snow levels fell below 5500 feet bringing heavy snow to areas south and east of Lake Tahoe. Snowfall totals south of Lake Tahoe were between 10 and 17 inches on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the eastern Pacific into the West on the 30th, bringing heavy snow and rain to the eastern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 14 inches along highway 395 from Lee Vining north. Near the Sierra crest, SNOTEL and Mammoth ski area sites indicated between 12 and 18 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in a shallow marine layer confined low to the coast, producing areas of dense fog along the Orange and San Diego County coasts between the 22nd and 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown Field municipal airport reported visibilities of a quarter mile from 1953 PST on the 22nd through 0253 PST on the 23rd.|Gillespie Field airport intermittently reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 2055 PST on the 22nd through 0335 PST on the 23rd.|Carlsbad McClellan-Palomar airport intermittently reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 1906-2038 PST on the 22nd and again between 0121-0409 PST and 0914-0940 PST on the 23rd.|Montgomery Field ASOS reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0624-0818 PST on the 23rd.|Red Beach ASOS intermittently reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0318-0701 PST on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Decreasing marine layer depth resulted in areas of dense fog along the coast, extending inland to the coastal mesas, on the morning of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fullerton municipal airport ASOS reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0425-0518 PST and again between 0621-0930 PST on the 28th.|Los Alamitos U.S. Army Airfield reported visibilities mostly a quarter mile or less between 0644-0930 PST on the 28th.

The circulation around the storm produced a southerly wind over the Tehachapi Mountains which funneled through Tejon Pass and down the Grapevine into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. (Garlock Road is prone to flooding from runoff from the El Paso Mountains.). In Dinuba, water covering the road was estimated at 8 to 12 inches deep. ||In the Southern Sierra Nevada, estimates of new snowfalls were as high as four feet. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of April, 2014. Extreme to Exceptional drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for almost the entire Central California region.  This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California.  In addition precipitation deficits began to mount as the region was nearing the end of the bulk of the rainy season with continued deficits.  As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

This was 0.08 inch greater than the normal of 2.03 inches and was the first time since December 2012 that Fresno had above-normal rainfall for a month.||The south end of the San Joaquin Valley was rain-shadowed, with Bakersfield receiving only 0.28 inch of rain, almost all of which fell from the second storm on February 28th. The second storm was located off the central California coast, and was impressive on satellite pictures with a tightly-wound structure and a clear center much like the eye of a tropical system. The circulation around the storm produced a southerly wind over the Tehachapi Mountains which funneled through Tejon Pass and down the Grapevine into the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Winds gusted as high as 89 mph at Grapevine Peak, and to 50 mph at Sunflower Valley on the west side of the Valley. At Buck Rock, in the Sierra Nevada in Tulare County, winds gusted to 66 mph.|Several thunderstorms were spawned by the storms, with the second system being the most potent. Numerous trees were toppled in both the San Joaquin Valley and the Sierra foothills by winds, in many cases pulling down power lines. One thunderstorm at Squaw Valley in the foothills produced a gust estimated at 65 mph, which toppled trees and damaged the roof of a garage. On the Valley floor, there were reports of houses and parked vehicles damaged by falling trees or large limbs. Near Fresno, a tree 3 feet in diameter was toppled.||Street flooding was reported at several locations, not only in the San Joaquin Valley but also in eastern Kern County where the Garlock Road flooded. (Garlock Road is prone to flooding from runoff from the El Paso Mountains.). In Dinuba, water covering the road was estimated at 8 to 12 inches deep. ||In the Southern Sierra Nevada, estimates of new snowfalls were as high as four feet. (These were Snotel observations; a post-storm snow survey had not been conducted at the time of this summary.)||February 2014 was much warmer than normal, and was Fresno���s warmest February on record. Bakersfield tied for its 6th warmest February on record.

A Pacific storm system moved across the Bay Area on February 28. It dropped several inches of rainfall and brought gusty winds to the area. This resulted in flooding of urban areas, small streams and creeks, and damage to power lines and trees as well as a few localized mud and rockslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company reporting that 2500 customers are without power in Napa at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across the Bay Area on February 28. It dropped several inches of rainfall and brought gusty winds to the area. This resulted in flooding of urban areas, small streams and creeks, and damage to power lines and trees as well as a few localized mud and rockslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic hazard due to downed tree and power lines near Bonny Dune Road and Smith Grade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another storm approached California on March 5th. This system brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts of Kern County on the 6th, with gusts to 55 mph recorded at Cache Creek and 53 mph at Indian Wells Canyon and the Mojave Air and Spaceport. March 6th also saw Fresno receive 0.02 inch of rain, the only measurable rain during the first 24 days of the month.||The second week of March saw high pressure return to California, bringing another round of warm, dry weather. An offshore flow developed over the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains on March 11th-12th, resulting in a ���mini-Mono��� event during the morning of the 12th. Trees and power poles were blown down near Oakhurst, and gusts of 50-55 mph were recorded from Hetch Hetchy near Yosemite south to Bear Valley Springs and Grapevine Peak in the Tehachapi Mountains.||The offshore flow also provided orographic forcing over the east slopes of the Southern Sierra Nevada. Clouds that formed east of the crest spilled over the summits into the Hanford warning/ forecast area. Isolated showers developed near Giant Forest, and while in-cloud lightning was detected with one of the cells, there was no cloud-to-ground lightning.

The Pierce Fire began around 1537 PST on the 15th near the intersection of Avenue 66 and Pierce Street in Thermal. Initially reported at 10 acres, the fire burned at a moderate rate of spread through medium to heavy brush, increasing rapidly to 200 acres over just a few hours. Road closures were in effect along north and southbound Highway 86 between Avenue 86 and Avenue 80, as well as Avenue 66 from Avenue 86 to Pierce Street. The fire burned a total of 350 acres before being fully contained at 2234 PST on the 15th. The fire downed power lines in the area and destroyed one outbuilding. No injuries to firefighters or civilians were reported.

The California drought continued in full force during the month of February, 2014. Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for much of the region, with exceptional drought detected along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. For California as a whole, 2013 was the driest year on record. As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

The Soda Wildfire was in Sequoia NF.  Ignition date was 1/13/14 and the cause was known.  Location was 20 miles E of Springville (Tulare County) and the size was 1274 acres.  Containment was likely reached when the fire received 9 inches of snow on Jan 30-31.  Cost to containment was $168,000.

A series of large, long-period northwest swells, generated by storms moving over the north-central Pacific brought high surf and strong rip currents to portions of the Orange and San Diego County coast during the week of January 20th. This event covers the third and largest of the swells to move through the coastal waters on the 24-26th. Areas north of Newport Beach in Orange County and south of Del Mar in San Diego County saw widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Huntington Beach lifeguards and Surfline reported consistent 6 to 8 foot surf at Huntington, Seal and Bolsa Chica State Beaches during the three-day period, with occasional sets to 10 feet, mainly on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to several areas around northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several RAWS stations reported gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust at the Juniper Creek RAWS was 61 mph at 15/1552 PST.   The peak gust at the Rush Creek RAWS was 60 mph at 15/1703 PST.   The peak gust at the Timber Mountain RAWS was 73 mph at 15/1503 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Decreasing marine layer depth resulted in areas of dense fog along the coast, extending inland to the coastal mesas, on the morning of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown Field municipal airport ASOS reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0146-0233 PST on the 28th.|Montgomery Field ASOS reported visibilites of a quarter mile or less between 0630-0900 PST on the 28th.|MCAS Miramar airport reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0652-0900 PST on the 28th.|Camp Pendleton MCAS ASOS reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0354-0651 PST on the 28th.|Oceanside Municipal Airport reported visibilities of a quarter mile between 0419-0623 PST on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an exceptional period with little to no rainfall, much needed rain fell across the Bay Area February 6th through the 9th. In fact, the lack of rainfall lead to the largest deficit of rain in recorded history for the Bay Area.||The first storm system began to develop on Wednesday (Feb 5) as a shortwave trough moved toward the West Coast. The shortwave trough deepened through the day allowing for a surface low to develop Wednesday Night off the coast. The resulting surface low increased low level warm advection and enhanced orographic lift along the coastal mountains. Rainfall amounts with this system varied from just over one half inch to a little more than two inches. ||The second and much stronger storm system brought impressive rainfall totals to the coastal mountains, especially the North Bay. The bulk of the rainfall occurred Friday night through Saturday. Several key factors led to the impressive rainfall: middle level flow perpendicular to the coast , orographic lift along the coastal mountains, but most prominently was a plume of very moist air streaming in from off the Pacific Ocean (an Atmospheric River).  As the moisture plume moved into the region rain began to overspread the North Bay Thursday night into Friday morning. Over the next 24 hours the main push of higher precipitable water moved over the Bay Area leading to the onset of steadier and at times heavy rainfall. By Sunday, rain began to diminish with off and on showers through the day.||Despite the long preceding period of dry weather, the several days of persistent rainfall resulted in some flooding and even landslides in parts of the Bay Area. Conditions were so bad on a stretch of the Ft Ross-Cazadero Highway that part of it washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded streets reported around the 2 AM Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms developed in and near the Chalfont Valley during the afternoon and evening of the 1st, bringing localized heavy rain, a minor mudslide over highway 6 (11 miles south of Benton), as well as some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated nickel-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed across southern Santa Barbara county. The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported sustained winds between 40 and 51 MPH with gusts between 59 and 66 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated gusty Santa Ana winds across sections of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains with gusts in excess of 65 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeasterly winds developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. The RAWS sensor at Camp Nine reported gusts between 60 and 66 MPH while the RAWS sensor at Chilao reported gusts between 59 and 69 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southwest winds developed across the Antelope Valley. The ASOS at Palmdale Airport reported sustained winds of 46 MPH with gusts to 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong west to northwest wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties as well as the Antelope Valley. In the mountains, northwesterly wind gusts up to 60 MPH were reported. Across the Antelope Valley, sustained winds up to 40 MPH were reported along with gusts in excess of 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Palmdale Airport reported sustained winds of 40 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January was an abnormally dry and warm month for interior Northern California. Many record high temperatures were broken, and a state-wide drought was declared on January 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A plethora of records were set across interior northern California for January. The following is from one of our oldest record sites, Downtown Sacramento. The highlights include: the longest dry period (52 days) during the rainy season from Dec. 8th, 2013 to Jan. 28th, 2014 that did not receive measurable rainfall, the third driest January in history since 1950 with 0.20 inches of rainfall which is only 5% of normal, highest January average maximum temperature of 66.1 degrees, new all-time record high for the month of January set on Jan. 24th of 79 degrees, broke the number of days with high temperatures of 70 degrees or higher with 3 days, record high temperatures were set on 12 different days, and lastly, everyday of January 2014 had a daytime high temperature that was above normal for the month for Downtown Sacramento.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated gusty Santa Ana winds across sections of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains with gusts in excess of 65 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeasterly winds developed across the mountains of Ventura county. Wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were reported in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated gusty Santa Ana winds across sections of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains with gusts in excess of 65 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed across the Santa Monica Mountains. An automated sensor at Laguna Peak reported wind gusts as high as 75 MPH. The RAWS sensor at Malibu Hills reported wind gusts between 58 and 65 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Colby Wildfire started in the hills above the San Gabriel Valley. The fire was fueled by the combination of an extended period of dry, northeast winds and very dry vegetation. The fire destroyed 5 homes and burned 1,952 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colby Wildfire burned 1,952 acres of land in the hills above the San Gabriel Valley. The fire was started by an illegal campfire and it destroyed 5 homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Energetic flow (a strong jet stream) from the Pacific Ocean brought high winds to the Surprise Valley on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Surprise Valley RAWS reported wind gusts as high as 71 mph on the 12th. Meanwhile, a home weather station in Fort Bidwell recorded wind gusts up to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern California early on the 16th. Ahead of the front on the 15th, strong winds occurred in the lee of the Warner and Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust up to 73 mph were noted at the Surprise Valley RAWS on the 15th. Meanwhile, a home weather station in Fort Bidwell recorded wind gusts up to 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern California early on the 16th. Ahead of the front on the 15th, strong winds occurred in the lee of the Warner and Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported along highway 395 south of Susanville on the 15th. Wind gusts up to 71 mph were noted at the Doyle RAWS with gusts up to 60 mph in Janesville. Five miles northwest of Susanville, power lines were reported down by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern California early on the 16th. Ahead of the front on the 15th, strong winds occurred in the lee of the Warner and Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts between 60 and 76 mph were indicated by RAWS stations between Coleville and Bridgeport as well as the Mammoth AWOS. Highway 395 was closed between Lee Vining and Bridgeport with two tractor trailer trucks blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an exceptional period with little to no rainfall, much needed rain fell across the Bay Area February 6th through the 9th. In fact, the lack of rainfall lead to the largest deficit of rain in recorded history for the Bay Area.||The first storm system began to develop on Wednesday (Feb 5) as a shortwave trough moved toward the West Coast. The shortwave trough deepened through the day allowing for a surface low to develop Wednesday Night off the coast. The resulting surface low increased low level warm advection and enhanced orographic lift along the coastal mountains. Rainfall amounts with this system varied from just over one half inch to a little more than two inches. ||The second and much stronger storm system brought impressive rainfall totals to the coastal mountains, especially the North Bay. The bulk of the rainfall occurred Friday night through Saturday. Several key factors led to the impressive rainfall: middle level flow perpendicular to the coast , orographic lift along the coastal mountains, but most prominently was a plume of very moist air streaming in from off the Pacific Ocean (an Atmospheric River).  As the moisture plume moved into the region rain began to overspread the North Bay Thursday night into Friday morning. Over the next 24 hours the main push of higher precipitable water moved over the Bay Area leading to the onset of steadier and at times heavy rainfall. By Sunday, rain began to diminish with off and on showers through the day.||Despite the long preceding period of dry weather, the several days of persistent rainfall resulted in some flooding and even landslides in parts of the Bay Area. Conditions were so bad on a stretch of the Ft Ross-Cazadero Highway that part of it washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported this as a 24 hour rainfall total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of the growing season greatly increased the impact of freezing temperatures, so freeze warnings for some of the west side valleys were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 23 to 28 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onset of the growing season greatly increased the impact of freezing temperatures, so freeze warnings for some of the west side valleys were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 25 to 33 degrees.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong west to northwest wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties as well as the Antelope Valley. In the mountains, northwesterly wind gusts up to 60 MPH were reported. Across the Antelope Valley, sustained winds up to 40 MPH were reported along with gusts in excess of 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Camp Nine reported northerly wind gusts up to 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong west to northwest wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties as well as the Antelope Valley. In the mountains, northwesterly wind gusts up to 60 MPH were reported. Across the Antelope Valley, sustained winds up to 40 MPH were reported along with gusts in excess of 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly winds, up to 60 MPH, were reported across eastern sections of the Ventura county mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the Antelope Valley. Some storms were strong enough to produce flash flooding across the community of Palmdale. In addition, one thunderstorm generated nickel-size hail in Palmdale which was just below severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in the Palmdale area reported a strong thunderstorm (with heavy rain) that produced flash flooding near the intersection of 47th Street East and East Avenue South. Elsewhere across the Antelope Valley, reports of flooding due to heavy rain were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The storm that reached the central California interior at the end of January heralded a week of unsettled weather for the region. February 1st saw low-level clouds banked against the San Joaquin Valley-facing slopes of the mountains in Kern and Tulare Counties, with clouds pushing into the Tehachapi Pass. The next day, a low-pressure system dropped south along the northern California coast, bringing precipitation to the region during the afternoon. This was a cold system, with the snow level down to around 3000 feet (and even lower in stronger snow showers). Yet despite the cold, unstable airmass, cloud cover inhibited thunderstorm development.||Despite the generally light rainfall amounts on the San Joaquin Valley floor, there was enough boundary-layer moisture to trigger patchy dense fog development in eastern Kings and western Tulare Counties (where the heaviest rain had fallen) around sunrise on February 3rd. Over the mountains, clouds rimmed the San Joaquin Valley and isolated showers were reported over the Southern Sierra Nevada.||Low temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley dropped below freezing during the morning of February 4th, with patchy frost reported in the coldest rural areas. These cold low temperatures continued the next morning, then clouds associated with the first major winter storm of the season arrived during the afternoon of the 5th.||The storm brought moderate to heavy rain to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on February 6th, except for the strongly rain-shadowed south end of the Valley. Fresno received 0.80 inch of rain on the 6th, nearly two thirds of the total for November through January of 1.26 inch. In sharp contrast, Bakersfield only recorded 0.04 inch, one twentieth of Fresno���s amount. Heavy snow fell in the Southern Sierra Nevada, with the snotel at Big Meadows receiving an estimated two feet of new snow, with Dana Meadows getting only a few inches less. ||Unsettled weather continued through February 10th. As upper-level impulses moved over the area, periods of light showers continued across the central California interior. However, rainfall amounts, even when totaled from February 7th through the 10th, were considerably less than on the 6th. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, an estimated 4 to 7 inches of additional snow fell above 9000 feet. Further south, a mix of rain and snow was reported on February 7th near Bear Valley Springs in the Tehachapi Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain and snow mix.  Heavy rain also occurred during a 24 hour period in Bear Valley Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of March, 2014. Extreme to Exceptional drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for almost the entire Central California region.  This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California.  In addition precipitation deficits began to mount as the region was nearing the end of the bulk of the rainy season with continued deficits.  As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of February, 2014. Extreme drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for much of the region, with exceptional drought detected along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. For California as a whole, 2013 was the driest year on record. As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HADS unit at Scott Mountain near Callahan at an elevation of 5899 feet recorded 6.2 inches of snow during this ten hour interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring front brought heavy snow to portions of Siskiyou county above 3500 feet in northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Shasta RAWS at an elevation of 3573 recorded 0.71 inches of liquid precipitation in 14 hours between 31/0454 PST and 31/1854 PST, of which 0.65 inches fell in the first 12 hours.  The HADS unit at Mount Shasta at an elevation of 3549 feet recorded 0.61 inches of liquid precipitation in 12.5 hours between 31/0500 PST and 31/1730 PST.  Based on these amounts and a known snow level just above these units, it is assumed that at least six inches of snow fell at higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring front brought heavy snow to portions of Siskiyou county above 3500 feet in northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Shasta RAWS at an elevation of 3573 recorded 0.71 inches of liquid precipitation in 14 hours between 31/0454 PST and 31/1854 PST, of which 0.65 inches fell in the first 12 hours.  The HADS unit at Mount Shasta at an elevation of 3549 feet recorded 0.61 inches of liquid precipitation in 12.5 hours between 31/0500 PST and 31/1730 PST.  Based on these amounts, a known snow level just above these units, and its proximity to CAZ083, it is assumed that at least six inches of snow fell at higher elevations in CAZ083.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northerly pressure gradient generated gusty northerly winds across sections of Southern California. The usual north wind-prone areas such as the Santa Ynez mountains and the Interstate 5 corridor through the Los Angeles county experienced wind gusts as high as 77 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed through the Santa Ynez mountains in southern Santa Barbara county. The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported northerly wind gusts as high as 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of April, 2014. Extreme to Exceptional drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for almost the entire Central California region.  This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California.  In addition precipitation deficits began to mount as the region was nearing the end of the bulk of the rainy season with continued deficits.  As the 2013-2014 winter progresses, little rain has fallen across California. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

Moisture from the rains saturated the airmass near the San Joaquin Valley floor, bringing the first episodes of Tule Fog of the winter. Of the four days (February 12th-15th) with locally dense fog, the 13th saw the most fog. Even so, the fog was mostly patchy and formed mainly in the Madera-Atwater and Selma-Hanford/Visalia areas. Dense fog also formed near daybreak along the Highway 41 corridor in western Fresno County and the Highway 198 corridor from Lemoore to Hanford.||In addition to the low-level moisture, fog development was aided by a stable airmass due to an upper-level ridge over the central California interior. This high pressure aloft also brought a warming trend, with above-normal temperatures from February 12th through the 17th.

An upper-level trough moved through southern California, strengthening over the Great Basin, resulting in strong, gusty southwest to west winds over the mountains and deserts the evening of the 25th, through the 26th. The majority of the high winds occurred in the San Diego and Riverside County mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Burns Canyon RAWS between 2000 and 2200 PST on the 25th. During this time, a peak gust of 67 mph was recorded between 2051 and 2151 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A teen from Lakewood drowned on the 14th off Sunset Beach. Damian Frierson Jr., 19, was pulled from the water by Huntington Beach city lifeguards and transported to Huntington Beach Hospital, where he died around 1821 PST, according to the Orange County coroner. It took about 25 lifeguards, 15 from the city and additional units from Bolsa Chica State Beach and Seal Beach, about 47 minutes to locate Frierson, who was found submerged in about 10 to 15 feet of water. At about 1643 PST, a lifeguard on duty told Frierson and two women with him to get out of the water because of the currents. A guard saw one of the women on shore yelling that her friends needed help and that he other woman was still in the rip current. CPR was initiated after Frierson was brought back to shore. It was the first drowning in Huntington Beach and Sunset Beach this year. Lifeguard reports from 4 PM that day at Huntington Beach reported surf of 4 feet, and reports from Surfline surf observers reported surf of 2-3 feet at 12:50 PM that afternoon. At around the time of the incident, the SIO (Scripps Institute of Oceanography) San Pedro Buoy reported a dominant 1.3 foot West swell at 15 seconds and the SIO Dana Point buoy reported a dominant 1.6 foot West-southwest swell at 17 seconds. Huntington Beach lifeguards reported 2-3 foot surf with no rip currents at 1030 PST on Sun April 13, and 2-3 foot surf with strong rip currents at 1530 PST on Tues April 15. No rip current report was sent out on the day of the incident (Mon April 14) for Huntington Beach, though it was later confirmed by lifeguards that a rip current was responsible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A teen from Lakewood drowned off Sunset Beach. Damian Frierson Jr., 19, was pulled from the water by Huntington Beach city lifeguards and transported to Huntington Beach Hospital, where he died at around 1821 PST. Drowning due to rip currents was suspected, and later confirmed to be the cause of death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northerly pressure gradient generated gusty northerly winds across sections of Southern California. The usual north wind-prone areas such as the Santa Ynez mountains and the Interstate 5 corridor through the Los Angeles county experienced wind gusts as high as 77 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed through and around the Interstate 5 corridor. The RAWS sensor at Whitaker Peak reported gusts as high as 77 MPH while the ASOS at Sandberg reported sustained winds between 40 and 45 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the end of February and into early March, a strong winter storm moved across Southern California. This storm generated heavy showers and thunderstorms which produced flash flooding and debris flows across Los Angeles county on the last day of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and debris flows were reported near the Colby burn scar in the San Gabriel Valley near Glendora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the end of February and into early March, a strong winter storm moved across Southern California. This storm generated heavy showers and thunderstorms which produced flash flooding and debris flows across Los Angeles county on the last day of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in Easley Canyon, near the Colby burn scar. Media reports indicated two garages were damaged by mud and debris flows induced by heavy showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the end of February and into early March, a strong winter storm moved across Southern California. This storm generated heavy showers and thunderstorms which produced flash flooding and debris flows across Los Angeles county on the last day of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding in the community of Lakeview Terrace. Two women were trapped when a wall of water swept across their driveway into the street.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated gusty northeast winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts as high as 62 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong Santa Ana winds developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. The RAWS sensor at Warm Springs reported northeast wind gusts between 60 and 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an exceptional period with little to no rainfall, much needed rain fell across the Bay Area February 6th through the 9th. In fact, the lack of rainfall lead to the largest deficit of rain in recorded history for the Bay Area.||The first storm system began to develop on Wednesday (Feb 5) as a shortwave trough moved toward the West Coast. The shortwave trough deepened through the day allowing for a surface low to develop Wednesday Night off the coast. The resulting surface low increased low level warm advection and enhanced orographic lift along the coastal mountains. Rainfall amounts with this system varied from just over one half inch to a little more than two inches. ||The second and much stronger storm system brought impressive rainfall totals to the coastal mountains, especially the North Bay. The bulk of the rainfall occurred Friday night through Saturday. Several key factors led to the impressive rainfall: middle level flow perpendicular to the coast , orographic lift along the coastal mountains, but most prominently was a plume of very moist air streaming in from off the Pacific Ocean (an Atmospheric River).  As the moisture plume moved into the region rain began to overspread the North Bay Thursday night into Friday morning. Over the next 24 hours the main push of higher precipitable water moved over the Bay Area leading to the onset of steadier and at times heavy rainfall. By Sunday, rain began to diminish with off and on showers through the day.||Despite the long preceding period of dry weather, the several days of persistent rainfall resulted in some flooding and even landslides in parts of the Bay Area. Conditions were so bad on a stretch of the Ft Ross-Cazadero Highway that part of it washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported this as a 24 hour rainfall total.

A powerful winter storm. which began to impact Southern California on the last day in February, continued to bring significant weather to the area through March 2nd. Rainfall totals were very impressive, ranging from 1 to 4 inches across coastal areas to between 6 and 12 inches across the mountains and foothills. This winter storm generated some intense showers which produced flash flooding and debris flows as well as some severe thunderstorms. In the mountains, the combination of 6 to 12 inches of snowfall and southerly wind gusts around 90 MPH generated winter storm conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms moved across the communities of Asuza and Glendora, generating flash flooding and debris flows. Law enforcement reported that several homes were damaged due to debris flows.

Moisture from the rains saturated the airmass near the San Joaquin Valley floor, bringing the first episodes of Tule Fog of the winter. Of the four days (February 12th-15th) with locally dense fog, the 13th saw the most fog. Even so, the fog was mostly patchy and formed mainly in the Madera-Atwater and Selma-Hanford/Visalia areas. Dense fog also formed near daybreak along the Highway 41 corridor in western Fresno County and the Highway 198 corridor from Lemoore to Hanford.||In addition to the low-level moisture, fog development was aided by a stable airmass due to an upper-level ridge over the central California interior. This high pressure aloft also brought a warming trend, with above-normal temperatures from February 12th through the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continued in full force during the month of June, 2014. Exceptional drought was detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor for almost the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The 2013-2014 water year (July 1 - June 30) concluded with Fresno setting its second driest on record (4.81 inches) and Bakersfield setting its third driest (2.41 inches). ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, a decrease in the snow-related tourism activity in the Southern Sierra Nevada, reduction in air quality due to persistent stagnant air, loss or reduction of ground water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. For the first time on record, red flag warnings were issued in January for not only the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains but also the south end of the San Joaquin Valley.

The Shirley wildfire started on May 13, 2014 and was contained on June 21, 2014. The fire was located 4 miles west of Wofford Heights in Kern County. It burned 2545 acres and 4 structures were lost. There were no fatalities. The cost of containment was $12.15 million.

Surface high pressure over the Great Basin brought very strong and gusty northeast to east winds and hot weather from the 12th through the 14th, before the winds finally decreased. The hardest hit areas were the mountains and western foothills of Riverside and San Diego County. The gusty winds downed countless trees, power lines and blew tiles from roofs. The winds also helped to fuel and spread nearly a dozen wildfires in San Diego County alone. The hot weather even spawned a damaging dust devil early on, before the winds really got going.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Big Black Mountain mesonet between 0107-0117 PST on the 14th and ended between 1037-1047 PST that morning. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east at 50-70 mph with gusts mostly over 65 mph, including the peak gust of 87 mph, recorded between 0537-0547 PST. High winds began at the Hellhole Canyon mesonet between 0640-0650 PST on the 14th and ended between 1030-1040 PST that morning. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east-northeast at 30-40 mph with gusts over 60 mph, including the peak gust of 75 mph, recorded between 0920-0930 PST. These winds eventually made it down to the Bonsall area, downing a power line. High winds began at the Alpine RAWS between 0352-0452 PST on the 14th and ended between 1052-1152 PST that morning. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east-northeast at 35-40 mph with gusts over 65 mph, including the peak gust of 69 mph, recorded between 0752-0852 PST. The Alpine Fire department reported part of a carport roof being blown off by the strong winds around 0930 PST. High winds began at the Dye Mountain mesonet between 00430-0440 PST on the 14th and ended between 0930-0940 PST that morning. During this time, winds were sustained out of the east-northeast at 35-40 mph with gusts off an on over 58 mph, including the peak gust of 63 mph, recorded between 0450-0500 PST. High winds occurred at the County Estates mesonet between 0910-0930 PST on the 14th with a peak gust of 64 mph out of the east during that time. A nearby spotter reported a 2.5 inch tree limb fell while wind had been gusting to 50 mph.
